// Define GPIO pins

#define BUTTON_GPIO 4

// Define log file name

// Global variables for signal handling
volatile sig_atomic_t program_running = 1;

// Function prototypes
int initialize_gpio();
void control_lock(int lock_gpio);
void log_event(int lock_id, const char *action);
void button_callback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick);
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Signal handler for graceful shutdown
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    if (signal == SIGINT) {
        printf("Caught SIGINT, exiting gracefully.\n");
        program_running = 0;
        gpioTerminate();
        exit(0);
    }
}

// Initialize GPIO pins
int initialize_gpio() {
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1; // Indicate failure
    }

    gpioSetMode(LOCK_1_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK_2_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_PUD_UP); // Enable pull-up resistor

    gpioWrite(LOCK_1_GPIO, 1); // Ensure locks are initially unlocked
    gpioWrite(LOCK_2_GPIO, 1);

    return 0; // Indicate success
}

// Control the state of a door lock
void control_lock(int lock_gpio) {
    if (gpioWrite(lock_gpio, 0) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error writing to GPIO %d\n", lock_gpio);
    }
    if (gpioWrite(lock_gpio, 1) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error writing to GPIO %d\n", lock_gpio);
    }
}

// Log an event to the log file
void log_event(int lock_id, const char *action) {
    FILE *fp = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error opening log file!\n");
        return;
    }

    time_t t = time(NULL);
    struct tm tm = *localtime(&t);
    fprintf(fp, "%d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d, Lock %d, %s\n",
            tm.tm_year + 1900, tm.tm_mon + 1, tm.tm_mday, tm.tm_hour, tm.tm_min, tm.tm_sec,
            lock_id, action);

    fclose(fp);
}

// Callback function for button press
void button_callback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick) {
    if (level == 0 && program_running) { // Button pressed (falling edge)
        printf("Button pressed!\n");
        if (gpio == BUTTON_GPIO) {
            control_lock(LOCK_1_GPIO);
            log_event(1, "Locked");
            gpioDelay(1000); // Keep lock locked for 1 second
            control_lock(LOCK_1_GPIO);
            log_event(1, "Unlocked");
        }
    }
}

int main() {
    // Install signal handler for SIGINT (Ctrl+C)
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Initialize GPIO
    if (initialize_gpio() != 0) {
        return 1;
    }

    // Set callback function for button press
    if (gpioSetAlertFuncEx(BUTTON_GPIO, button_callback, NULL) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error setting callback function!\n");
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    printf("Door lock control program started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    // Keep the program running until a signal is received
    while (program_running) {
        gpioDelay(100); // Reduce CPU usage
    }

    gpioTerminate();
    return 0;
}

<s><s>[INST]  Write a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W using the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks (connected to GPIO 17 and 27), triggered by a single momentary push button connected to GPIO 4 with debouncing; include robust error handling for GPIO initialization and save a log of all lock events (timestamp, lock ID, action) to `lock_events.log`.

1. Install the `wiringPi` and `pigpio` libraries if not already installed:

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install wiringPi
sudo pigpiod
```

2. Create a new C file, e.g., `door_lock_controller.c`:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define LOCK_1_PIN 17
#define LOCK_2_PIN 27
#define BUTTON_PIN 4
#define DEBOUNCE_DELAY 50

#define LOG_FILE "lock_events.log"

void log_event(int lock_id, int action) {
    time_t rawtime;
    struct tm *timeinfo;
    char time_str[80];
    FILE *log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");

    if (log_file == NULL) {
        perror("Failed to open log file");
        return;
    }

    time(&rawtime);
    timeinfo = localtime(&rawtime);

    strftime(time_str, sizeof(time_str), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", timeinfo);
    fprintf(log_file, "%s, Lock %d, %s\n", time_str, lock_id, (action == 1) ? "unlocked" : "locked");
    fclose(log_file);
}

void debounce_button(int pin, int *state) {
    static int last_state = -1;
    static unsigned long last_debounce = 0;
    unsigned long current_time = hal_millis();

    if (current_time - last_debounce > DEBOUNCE_DELAY) {
        last_debounce = current_time;

        if (*state != GPIOread(pin)) {
            *state = GPIOread(pin);
            last_state = *state;
        }
    }
}

int main() {
    int lock_1_state = 0;
    int lock_2_state = 0;
    int button_state = 0;

    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        perror("Failed to initialize pigpio");
        return 1;
    }

    if (gpioSetMode(BUTTON_PIN, PI_INPUT) < 0 ||
        gpioSetMode(LOCK_1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT) < 0 ||
        gpioSetMode(LOCK_2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT)) {
        perror("Failed to set GPIO modes");
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    while (1) {
        debounce_button(BUTTON_PIN, &button_state);

        if (button_state != last_state) {
            if (button_state) {
                if (!lock_1_state) {
                    gpioWrite(LOCK_1_PIN, 1);
                    lock_1_state = 1;
                    log_event(1, 1);
                } else if (lock_2_state) {
                    gpioWrite(LOCK_2_PIN, 0);
                    lock_2_state = 0;
                    log_event(2, 0);
                }
            }
            last_state = button_state;
        }

        usleep(100000); // Sleep for 100ms to reduce CPU usage
    }

    gpioTerminate();
    return 0;
}
```

3. Compile and run the program:

```bash
gcc door_lock_controller.c -o door_lock_controller -lwiringPi -lpigpio
./door_lock_controller
```

prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W using the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks (connected to GPIO 17 and 27), triggered by a single momentary push button connected to GPIO 4 and a Passive Infrared (PIR) motion sensor connected to GPIO 22. Implement debouncing for the push button using `pigpio`'s callback features. The program should adopt a functional programming style, suitable for a beginner-level understanding, and designed for an interactive art installation.

Specifically:

1.  **GPIO Control:** Use `pigpio` functions (e.g., `gpioInitialise()`, `gpioSetMode()`, `gpioWrite()`, `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()`) to manage the GPIO pins. Initialize GPIO 17 and 27 as outputs for the door locks, GPIO 4 as an input with a pull-up resistor for the push button, and GPIO 22 as an input for the PIR sensor.

2.  **Button Debouncing:** Implement debouncing for the push button using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()` on GPIO 4. This function should call a separate function when the button state changes, which handles the logic for triggering the door locks.

3.  **Motion Sensor Integration:** Monitor GPIO 22 (PIR sensor) using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()`. When motion is detected (GPIO 22 goes HIGH), trigger a separate function to log the event.

4.  **Door Lock Control Logic:**
    *   A press of the button should toggle the state of *both* door locks.
    *   Motion detected by the PIR sensor should *only* unlock both door locks.
    *   Each door lock should be activated for a duration of 3 seconds. After 3 seconds, the lock should automatically deactivate. Use `pigpio`'s `gpioDelay()` function for timing.

5.  **Logging:** Create a log file named `lock_events.log`. Each lock event (button press, motion detected, lock activation, lock deactivation) should be logged with a timestamp, lock ID (17 or 27), and action (e.g., "Button Press", "Motion Detected", "Lock 17 Activated", "Lock 27 Deactivated"). Use standard C library functions like `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, `time()`, and `localtime()` for file I/O and timestamp generation.

6.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling. Check the return values of `gpioInitialise()` and `gpioSetMode()` and log any errors to `lock_events.log` and `stderr`. Include checks for file operations (e.g., `fopen()`). If `gpioInitialise()` fails, the program should exit gracefully with an error message.

7.  **Functional Programming Style:** Structure the code into small, reusable functions. For example:
    *   `initGPIO()`: Initializes all GPIO pins.
    *   `toggleLocks()`: Toggles the state of both door locks.
    *   `activateLock(int gpio, int duration_ms)`: Activates a specific door lock for a given duration.
    *   `logEvent(int gpio, const char *action)`: Logs an event to `lock_events.log`.
    *   `buttonCallback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)`: Handles the button press event.
    *   `motionCallback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)`: Handles the motion detection event.

8.  **Signal Handling:** Implement signal handling for `SIGINT` (Ctrl+C) to ensure that `gpioTerminate()` is called before the program exits, releasing the GPIO resources. Use `signal()` for signal handling.

9.  **Compilation:** The program should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag (e.g., `gcc -o door_control door_control.c -lpigpio -pthread`).

10. **Libraries:** Strictly use `pigpio`, `stdio.h`, `stdlib.h`, `time.h`, and `signal.h`. Do not use any C++ libraries or non-standard/proprietary libraries.

11. **Adherence to Standards:** Ensure all function calls and library usage are compatible with Raspberry Pi OS (Bookworm/Bullseye) and avoid deprecated features.

12. **Context:** Remember this is for an interactive art installation, so consider the user experience (e.g., clear logging, reliable operation).

<s><s>[INST]  Write a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W using the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks (connected to GPIO 17 and 27), triggered by a single momentary push button connected to GPIO 4 with debouncing; include robust error handling for GPIO initialization and save a log of all lock events (timestamp, lock ID, action) to `lock_events.log`.
 [/INST] To create a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W that controls two 12V electromagnetic door locks using the `pigpio` library, connects a single momentary push button to GPIO 4 for triggering, and logs all lock events to `lock_events.log`, follow the steps below:

1. Install the `wiringPi` and `pigpio` libraries:

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install wiringpi
sudo apt-get install pigpio
```

2. Create a new file called `door_lock_controller.c` and paste the following code:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define LOCK_1_GPIO 17
#define LOCK_2_GPIO 27
#define BUTTON_GPIO 4
#define BAUD_RATE 115200

static const char *lock_names[2] = {"Lock 1", "Lock 2"};
static const char *actions[2] = {"locked", "unlocked"};

void log_event(int lock_id, const char *action) {
    time_t rawtime;
    struct tm *time_info;
    char time_str[80];

    time(&rawtime);
    time_info = localtime(&rawtime);

    strftime(time_str, sizeof(time_str), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", time_info);
    printf("%s,%d,%s,%s\n", time_str, lock_id + 1, lock_names[lock_id], action);
    fflush(stdout);

    FILE *log_file = fopen("lock_events.log", "a");
    if (log_file) {
        fprintf(log_file, "%s,%d,%s,%s\n", time_str, lock_id + 1, lock_names[lock_id], action);
        fclose(log_file);
    }
}

void debounce_button(int pin) {
    int state = digitalRead(pin);
    usleep(100000); // 100ms debounce delay

    if (state == 1) {
        usleep(100000); // 100ms debounce delay
        if (digitalRead(pin) == 1)
            return;
    }

    printf("Button pressed.\n");
    log_event(-1, "pressed");
}

void control_lock(int lock_id, int action) {
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        perror("Failed to initialize pigpio library");
        exit(1);
    }

    if (gpioSetMode(LOCK_1_GPIO + lock_id, PI_OUTPUT) < 0) {
        perror("Failed to set GPIO mode");
        gpioTerminate();
        exit(1);
    }

    if (action == 1) {
        gpioWrite(LOCK_1_GPIO + lock_id, 1);
        usleep(1000000); // 1s duration for locking
        gpioWrite(LOCK_1_GPIO + lock_id, 0);
    } else if (action == 0) {
        gpioWrite(LOCK_1_GPIO + lock_id, 1);
        usleep(50000); // 50ms duration for unlocking
        gpioWrite(LOCK_1_GPIO + lock_id, 0);
    }

    gpioTerminate();
}

int main() {
    wiringPiSetupGpio();

    pinMode(BUTTON_GPIO, INPUT);
    pullUpDnControl(BUTTON_GPIO, PUD_UP);

    while (1) {
        debounce_button(BUTTON_GPIO);

        if (digitalRead(BUTTON_GPIO) == 0) {
            int lock_id = -1;
            for (int i = 0; i < 2; ++i) {
                control_lock(i, 0);
                usleep(100000); // 100ms delay between lock operations
                control_lock(i, 1);
                log_event(i, "cycled");
            }
        }

        usleep(100000); // 100ms delay between button checks
    }

    return 0;
}
```

3. Compile the program using gcc:

```bash
gcc -o door_lock_controller door_lock_controller.c -lwiringPi -lpigpio -lrt
```

4. Run the compiled program:

```bash
sudo ./door_lock_controller
```

This program initializes the GPIO pins for the locks, button, and sets up the serial communication. It then enters an infinite loop, where it debounces the push button, and when the button is pressed, it cycles the two locks by locking and unlocking them. The lock events are logged to `lock_events.log`.</s>

                control_lock(LOCK_1_ID, 1); // Lock 1
                control_lock(LOCK_2_ID, 0); // Unlock 2

                log_event(LOCK_1_ID, 1);
                log_event(LOCK_2_ID, 0);

                last_button_press_time = current_time;
            }
        } else {
            last_button_press_time = 0; // Reset last press time if button is released
        }

        gpioDelay(10); // Small delay to reduce CPU usage
    }

    return 0;
}

// Function to initialize GPIO pins
int initialize_gpio() {
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    gpioSetMode(LOCK_1_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK_2_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_PUD_UP); // Enable pull-up resistor

    return 0;
}

// Function to read the button state
int read_button_state() {
    return gpioRead(BUTTON_GPIO);
}

// Function to control the door locks
void control_lock(int lock_id, int action) {
    int gpio_pin;
    if (lock_id == LOCK_1_ID) {
        gpio_pin = LOCK_1_GPIO;
    } else if (lock_id == LOCK_2_ID) {
        gpio_pin = LOCK_2_GPIO;
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid lock ID: %d\n", lock_id);
        return;
    }

    if (action == 1) { // Lock
        gpioWrite(gpio_pin, 1); // HIGH to lock
        printf("Lock %d locked.\n", lock_id);
    } else if (action == 0) { // Unlock
        gpioWrite(gpio_pin, 0); // LOW to unlock
        printf("Lock %d unlocked.\n", lock_id);
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid action: %d for lock %d\n", action, lock_id);
    }
}

// Function to log lock events
void log_event(int lock_id, int action) {
    FILE *fp = fopen("lock_events.log", "a");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error opening log file!\n");
        return;
    }

    time_t t = time(NULL);
    struct tm tm = *localtime(&t);
    char timestamp[20];
    strftime(timestamp, sizeof(timestamp), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", &tm);

    fprintf(fp, "%s - Lock %d %s\n", timestamp, lock_id, action ? "locked" : "unlocked");
    fclose(fp);
}

// Function to cleanup GPIO pins
void cleanup_gpio() {
    gpioWrite(LOCK_1_GPIO, 0); // Ensure locks are unlocked
    gpioWrite(LOCK_2_GPIO, 0);
    gpioTerminate();
}

// Signal handler for graceful shutdown
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("Caught signal %d, cleaning up...\n", signal);
    cleanup_gpio();
    exit(0);
}

input: Write a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W using the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks (connected to GPIO 17 and 27), triggered by a single momentary push button connected to GPIO 4 with debouncing; include robust error handling for GPIO initialization and save a log of all lock events (timestamp, lock ID, action) to `lock_events.log`.
prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W using the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks (connected to GPIO 17 and 27), triggered by a single momentary push button connected to GPIO 4 and a Passive Infrared (PIR) motion sensor connected to GPIO 22. Implement debouncing for the push button using `pigpio`'s callback features. The program should adopt a functional programming style, suitable for a beginner-level understanding, and designed for an interactive art installation.

Specifically:

1.  **GPIO Control:** Use `pigpio` functions (e.g., `gpioInitialise()`, `gpioSetMode()`, `gpioWrite()`, `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()`) to manage the GPIO pins. Initialize GPIO 17 and 27 as outputs for the door locks, GPIO 4 as an input with a pull-up resistor for the push button, and GPIO 22 as an input for the PIR sensor.

2.  **Button Debouncing:** Implement debouncing for the push button using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()` on GPIO 4. This function should call a separate function when the button state changes, which handles the logic for triggering the door locks.

3.  **Motion Sensor Integration:** Monitor GPIO 22 (PIR sensor) using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()`. When motion is detected (GPIO 22 goes HIGH), trigger a separate function to log the event.

4.  **Door Lock Control Logic:**
    *   A press of the button should toggle the state of *both* door locks.
    *   Motion detected by the PIR sensor should *only* unlock both door locks.
    *   Each door lock should be activated for a duration of 3 seconds. After 3 seconds, the lock should automatically deactivate. Use `pigpio`'s `gpioDelay()` function for timing.

5.  **Logging:** Create a log file named `lock_events.log`. Each lock event (button press, motion detected, lock activation, lock deactivation) should be logged with a timestamp, lock ID (17 or 27), and action (e.g., "Button Press", "Motion Detected", "Lock 17 Activated", "Lock 27 Deactivated"). Use standard C library functions like `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, `time()`, and `localtime()` for file I/O and timestamp generation.

6.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling. Check the return values of `gpioInitialise()` and `gpioSetMode()` and log any errors to `lock_events.log` and `stderr`. Include checks for file operations (e.g., `fopen()`). If `gpioInitialise()` fails, the program should exit gracefully with an error message.

7.  **Functional Programming Style:** Structure the code into small, reusable functions. For example:
    *   `initGPIO()`: Initializes all GPIO pins.
    *   `toggleLocks()`: Toggles the state of both door locks.
    *   `activateLock(int gpio, int duration_ms)`: Activates a specific door lock for a given duration.
    *   `logEvent(int gpio, const char *action)`: Logs an event to `lock_events.log`.
    *   `buttonCallback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)`: Handles the button press event.
    *   `motionCallback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)`: Handles the motion detection event.

8.  **Signal Handling:** Implement signal handling for `SIGINT` (Ctrl+C) to ensure that `gpioTerminate()` is called before the program exits, releasing the GPIO resources. Use `signal()` for signal handling.

9.  **Compilation:** The program should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag (e.g., `gcc -o door_control door_control.c -lpigpio -pthread`).

10. **Libraries:** Strictly use `pigpio`, `stdio.h`, `stdlib.h`, `time.h`, and `signal.h`. Do not use any C++ libraries or non-standard/proprietary libraries.

11. **Adherence to Standards:** Ensure all function calls and library usage are compatible with Raspberry Pi OS (Bookworm/Bullseye) and avoid deprecated features.

12. **Context:** Remember this is for an interactive art installation, so consider the user experience (e.g., clear logging, reliable operation).
explanation: The code initializes GPIO pins for door locks, a button, and a PIR sensor using the `pigpio` library. It implements debouncing for the button and uses callbacks for button presses and motion detection.  Lock events are logged to a file, and signal handling ensures proper cleanup upon program termination.

<s><s>[INST]  Write a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W using the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks (connected to GPIO 17 and 27), triggered by a single momentary push button connected to GPIO 4 with debouncing; include robust error handling for GPIO initialization and save a log of all lock events (timestamp, lock ID, action) to `lock_events.log`.
 [/INST] To create a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W that controls two 12V electromagnetic door locks using the `pigpio` library, you'll need to install the `wiringPi` and `pigpio` libraries first. You can do this by running the following commands:

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install wiringPi
sudo pigpio --update
sudo apt-get install pigpio
```

Here's a sample C program that achieves the requirements you've mentioned:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define LOCK_1_GPIO 17
#define LOCK_2_GPIO 27
#define BUTTON_GPIO 4
#define LOG_FILE "lock_events.log"

typedef struct {
    int lock_id;
    char action[10];
    time_t timestamp;
} LockEvent;

void log_event(int lock_id, char *action) {
    time_t rawtime;
    struct tm *timeinfo;
    char time_string[80];

    time(&rawtime);
    timeinfo = localtime(&rawtime);

    strftime(time_string, sizeof(time_string), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", timeinfo);

    FILE *log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (log_file == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening log file");
        return;
    }

    fprintf(log_file, "%s, %d, %s\n", time_string, lock_id, action);
    fclose(log_file);
}

int main(void) {
    int ret, i;
    static int lock_states[2] = {0, 0};
    static unsigned int button_edge = 0;
    pig_gpio_state_t button_state;

    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to initialize pigpio library\n");
        return 1;
    }

    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUp(BUTTON_GPIO, 1);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK_1_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK_2_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);

    while (1) {
        button_state = gpioRead(BUTTON_GPIO);

        if (button_state == button_edge) {
            // Debounce delay
            usleep(10000);

            button_state = gpioRead(BUTTON_GPIO);
            if (button_state != button_edge) {
                button_edge = !button_edge;

                for (i = 0; i < 2; ++i) {
                    if (lock_states[i]) {
                        gpioWrite(LOCK_1_GPIO + i, 0);
                        strcpy(lock_states[i].action, "unlatch");
                        log_event(i + 1, lock_states[i].action);
                        lock_states[i] = 0;
                    } else {
                        gpioWrite(LOCK_1_GPIO + i, 1);
                        strcpy(lock_states[i].action, "latch");
                        log_event(i + 1, lock_states[i].action);
                        lock_states[i] = 1;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }

    gpioTerminate();
    return 0;
}
```

This program initializes the `pigpio` library, sets the GPIO pins as inputs, outputs, and pull-ups for the buttons and locks, and enters an infinite loop to read the button state and control the locks accordingly. The program uses debouncing by delaying after detecting a button edge and then checks again to confirm the button state. When the button is pressed, it toggles the state of the corresponding lock. The lock state and action are saved to a log file named `lock_events.log`.

To compile the program, you can use the following command:

```bash
gcc -o door_lock door_lock.c -lwiringPi -lpthread -lrt -lrt -lpigpio
```

Then, run the compiled program with:

```bash
sudo ./door_lock
```</s>

input: Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C, reading proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index, logging the data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file; implement robust error handling for I2C communication failures and initialize with `wiringPiI2CSetup` for the Si1153 I2C address, simulating a smart window system adjusting tint based on UV.
prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the pigpio library to interface with the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C. The program should implement a motion-triggered surveillance system, logging proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file only when motion is detected by a PIR sensor connected to GPIO4.

Specifically:

1.  **Initialization:** Initialize the pigpio library using `gpioInitialise()`. If initialization fails, exit gracefully with an error message. Configure GPIO4 as an input using `gpioSetMode(4, PI_INPUT)`. Configure the Si1153 sensor using `i2cOpen()` with the appropriate I2C bus (typically 1) and the Si1153's I2C address (0x60). Implement error checking after `i2cOpen()` to ensure successful connection to the sensor.

2.  **Sensor Reading Functions:** Implement three functions to read the sensor data using a functional programming style (avoiding global state modification within the functions themselves).
    *   `readProximity(int handle)`: Reads the proximity value from the Si1153 using `i2cReadByteData()` with the appropriate register address. Handle potential I2C errors using `i2cReadByteData()` return value (negative values indicate errors).
    *   `readALS(int handle)`: Reads the ambient light sensor value from the Si1153 using `i2cReadWordData()` (or two `i2cReadByteData()` calls if necessary based on the sensor's register layout) and combines the bytes into a single 16-bit value. Handle potential I2C errors.
    *   `readUVIndex(int handle)`: Reads the UV index from the Si1153 using `i2cReadByteData()` with the appropriate register address. Handle potential I2C errors.

3.  **Motion Detection:** Continuously monitor the PIR sensor connected to GPIO4 using `gpioRead(4)`. If `gpioRead(4)` returns 1 (motion detected), proceed to read and log the sensor data.

4.  **Data Logging:** When motion is detected, get the current timestamp using `time()` and `localtime()`. Format the timestamp string using `strftime()`. Open the "sensor_log.txt" file in append mode ("a") using `fopen()`. Write the timestamp, proximity value, ALS value, and UV index to the file in a comma-separated format, using `fprintf()`. Close the file using `fclose()`. Include error handling for file operations (check the return value of `fopen()` and `fprintf()`).

5.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling for all I2C communication failures. If any `i2cReadByteData()` or `i2cReadWordData()` call returns a negative value, log an error message to the console using `fprintf(stderr, ...)` and attempt to re-initialize the I2C connection. If re-initialization fails after a few attempts, exit the program gracefully. Implement error handling for `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, and `fclose()` to ensure data integrity.

6.  **Cleanup:** Implement signal handling (e.g., using `signal(SIGINT, ...)` for Ctrl+C) to ensure proper cleanup before exiting. In the signal handler, close the I2C connection using `i2cClose()`, terminate the pigpio library using `gpioTerminate()`, and exit the program using `exit(0)`.

7.  **Functional Style:** The `readProximity`, `readALS`, and `readUVIndex` functions should operate without side effects (other than I/O). They should receive the I2C handle as an argument and return the sensor reading as a value.  Avoid global variables within these functions.

8.  **Standalone Approach:** The program should be self-contained and not rely on external configuration files or scripts. All necessary parameters (I2C address, GPIO pins) should be hardcoded or defined as constants.

9.  **Library Usage:** Use only the pigpio library and standard C libraries (stdio.h, stdlib.h, time.h, signal.h).

10. **Smart Window Tint Simulation (Simplified):** Although the original prompt mentioned a smart window, this will be simplified for this implementation.  After logging the data, print to the console a message indicating a simulated tint level adjustment based on the UV index. For example, if the UV index is above a certain threshold (e.g., 5), print "Simulating window tint darkening due to high UV index." This is a placeholder and does not require actual window tint control.

11. **Compilation:** The code should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag to link the pigpio library.
explanation: The code initializes the pigpio library, configures the PIR sensor GPIO as input, and opens an I2C connection to the Si1153 sensor. It continuously monitors the PIR sensor for motion. When motion is detected, it reads proximity, ALS, and UV index values from the Si1153 sensor, logs the data to a file with a timestamp, and simulates window tint adjustment based on the UV index. The code includes error handling for I2C communication and file operations, and it uses signal handling for graceful shutdown.

<s><s>[INST]  Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C, reading proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index, logging the data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file; implement robust error handling for I2C communication failures and initialize with `wiringPiI2CSetup` for the Si1153 I2C address, simulating a smart window system adjusting tint based on UV.
 [/INST] I cannot write and test the entire program for you, but I can provide you with a basic outline and some code snippets to help you get started on your project. This code is written in C and uses the wiringPi library for I2C communication.

First, make sure you have the wiringPi library installed on your Raspberry Pi:

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install wiringPi
```

Now, let's create the C program:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <sys/ioctl.h>
#include <linux/i2c-dev.h>
#include <wiringPiI2C.h>
#include <time.h>

#define SI1153_ADDR 0x62 // Si1153 I2C address

int file;

void log_data(float proximity, float als, float uv) {
    time_t rawtime;
    struct tm *timeinfo;
    char timestamp[20];

    time(&rawtime);
    timeinfo = localtime(&rawtime);

    strftime(timestamp, sizeof(timestamp), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", timeinfo);
    printf("%s - Proximity: %.2f, ALS: %.2f, UV: %.2f\n", timestamp, proximity, als, uv);

    file = open("sensor_log.txt", O_WRONLY | O_APPEND);
    if (file < 0) {
        perror("Error opening file");
        return;
    }

    dprintf(file, "%s - Proximity: %.2f, ALS: %.2f, UV: %.2f\n", timestamp, proximity, als, uv);
    close(file);
}

int main() {
    int fd;
    float proximity, als, uv;

int read_sensor_data(int i2c_handle, uint8_t reg_addr) {
    int data = i2cReadWordData(i2c_handle, reg_addr);
    if (data < 0) {

        return -1;

    return data;
}

// Function to initialize the Si1153 sensor
int init_sensor(int i2c_handle) {
    // Reset the sensor (optional, but recommended)
    i2cWriteByteData(i2c_handle, 0x07, 0x01);
    gpioDelay(10);

    // Enable ALS, Proximity, and UV sensors (example configuration)
    i2cWriteByteData(i2c_handle, 0x18, 0x0F); // Enable ALS
    i2cWriteByteData(i2c_handle, 0x19, 0x07); // Enable Proximity
    i2cWriteByteData(i2c_handle, 0x1A, 0x03); // Enable UV

    // Check the Part ID
    int part_id = read_sensor_data(i2c_handle, SI1153_REG_PART_ID);
    if (part_id < 0) {
        return -1;
    }
    if (part_id != 0x41) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error: Incorrect Part ID: 0x%02X\n", part_id);
        return -1;
    }

    printf("Sensor initialized successfully.\n");
    return 0;
}

// Function to read the sensor data
int read_sensor_data_all(int i2c_handle, uint16_t *als, uint16_t *proximity, uint16_t *uv_index) {
    int als_low = read_sensor_data(i2c_handle, SI1153_REG_ALS_VIS_DATA0);
    int als_high = read_sensor_data(i2c_handle, SI1153_REG_ALS_VIS_DATA1);
    int prox_low = read_sensor_data(i2c_handle, SI1153_REG_PS1_DATA0);
    int prox_high = read_sensor_data(i2c_handle, SI1153_REG_PS1_DATA1);
    int uv_low = read_sensor_data(i2c_handle, SI1153_REG_UV_INDEX0);
    int uv_high = read_sensor_data(i2c_handle, SI1153_REG_UV_INDEX1);

    if (als_low < 0 || als_high < 0 || prox_low < 0 || prox_high < 0 || uv_low < 0 || uv_high < 0) {
        return -1;
    }

    *als = (als_high << 8) | als_low;
    *proximity = (prox_high << 8) | prox_low;

void log_data(uint16_t als, uint16_t proximity, uint16_t uv_index) {

input: Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C, reading proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index, logging the data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file; implement robust error handling for I2C communication failures and initialize with `wiringPiI2CSetup` for the Si1153 I2C address, simulating a smart window system adjusting tint based on UV.
prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the pigpio library to interface with the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C. The program should implement a motion-triggered surveillance system, logging proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file only when motion is detected by a PIR sensor connected to GPIO4.

Specifically:

1.  **Initialization:** Initialize the pigpio library using `gpioInitialise()`. If initialization fails, exit gracefully with an error message. Configure GPIO4 as an input using `gpioSetMode(4, PI_INPUT)`. Configure the Si1153 sensor using `i2cOpen()` with the appropriate I2C bus (typically 1) and the Si1153's I2C address (0x60). Implement error checking after `i2cOpen()` to ensure successful connection to the sensor.

2.  **Sensor Reading Functions:** Implement three functions to read the sensor data using a functional programming style (avoiding global state modification within the functions themselves).
    *   `readProximity(int handle)`: Reads the proximity value from the Si1153 using `i2cReadByteData()` with the appropriate register address. Handle potential I2C errors using `i2cReadByteData()` return value (negative values indicate errors).
    *   `readALS(int handle)`: Reads the ambient light sensor value from the Si1153 using `i2cReadWordData()` (or two `i2cReadByteData()` calls if necessary based on the sensor's register layout) and combines the bytes into a single 16-bit value. Handle potential I2C errors.
    *   `readUVIndex(int handle)`: Reads the UV index from the Si1153 using `i2cReadByteData()` with the appropriate register address. Handle potential I2C errors.

3.  **Motion Detection:** Continuously monitor the PIR sensor connected to GPIO4 using `gpioRead(4)`. If `gpioRead(4)` returns 1 (motion detected), proceed to read and log the sensor data.

4.  **Data Logging:** When motion is detected, get the current timestamp using `time()` and `localtime()`. Format the timestamp string using `strftime()`. Open the "sensor_log.txt" file in append mode ("a") using `fopen()`. Write the timestamp, proximity value, ALS value, and UV index to the file in a comma-separated format, using `fprintf()`. Close the file using `fclose()`. Include error handling for file operations (check the return value of `fopen()` and `fprintf()`).

5.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling for all I2C communication failures. If any `i2cReadByteData()` or `i2cReadWordData()` call returns a negative value, log an error message to the console using `fprintf(stderr, ...)` and attempt to re-initialize the I2C connection. If re-initialization fails after a few attempts, exit the program gracefully. Implement error handling for `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, and `fclose()` to ensure data integrity.

6.  **Cleanup:** Implement signal handling (e.g., using `signal(SIGINT, ...)` for Ctrl+C) to ensure proper cleanup before exiting. In the signal handler, close the I2C connection using `i2cClose()`, terminate the pigpio library using `gpioTerminate()`, and exit the program using `exit(0)`.

7.  **Functional Style:** The `readProximity`, `readALS`, and `readUVIndex` functions should operate without side effects (other than I/O). They should receive the I2C handle as an argument and return the sensor reading as a value.  Avoid global variables within these functions.

8.  **Standalone Approach:** The program should be self-contained and not rely on external configuration files or scripts. All necessary parameters (I2C address, GPIO pins) should be hardcoded or defined as constants.

9.  **Library Usage:** Use only the pigpio library and standard C libraries (stdio.h, stdlib.h, time.h, signal.h).

10. **Smart Window Tint Simulation (Simplified):** Although the original prompt mentioned a smart window, this will be simplified for this implementation.  After logging the data, print to the console a message indicating a simulated tint level adjustment based on the UV index. For example, if the UV index is above a certain threshold (e.g., 5), print "Simulating window tint darkening due to high UV index." This is a placeholder and does not require actual window tint control.

11. **Compilation:** The code should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag to link the pigpio library.
explanation: The code initializes the pigpio library, configures the PIR sensor GPIO as input, and opens an I2C connection to the Si1153 sensor. It continuously monitors the PIR sensor for motion. When motion is detected, it reads proximity, ALS, and UV index values from the Si1153 sensor, logs the data to a file with a timestamp, and simulates window tint adjustment based on the UV index. The code includes error handling for I2C communication and file operations, and it uses signal handling for graceful shutdown.

<s><s>[INST]  Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C, reading proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index, logging the data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file; implement robust error handling for I2C communication failures and initialize with `wiringPiI2CSetup` for the Si1153 I2C address, simulating a smart window system adjusting tint based on UV.
 [/INST] To create a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C, logging data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file, and simulating a smart window system adjusting tint based on UV index, follow the steps below. First, make sure you have the wiringPi library installed:

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install wiringPi
```

Next, create a new C file named `si1153_sensor.c` and paste the following code:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <sys/ioctl.h>
#include <linux/i2c-dev.h>
#include <wiringPiI2C.h>
#include <time.h>

#define SI1153_I2C_ADDR 0x62

int file_fd, i2c_fd;

void log_data(char* data) {
    time_t rawtime;
    struct tm * timeinfo;
    char time_string[80];

    time(&rawtime);
    timeinfo = localtime(&rawtime);

    strftime(time_string, sizeof(time_string), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", timeinfo);
    FILE *log_file = fopen("sensor_log.txt", "a");
    fprintf(log_file, "%s %s\n", time_string, data);
    fclose(log_file);
}

int si1153_read_data(uint16_t *proximity, uint16_t *alas, uint16_t *uv) {
    uint8_t data[6];

    if (wiringPiI2CWriteReg16(i2c_fd, 0x80, 0x01) < 0) {
        perror("Error writing to Si1153");
        return -1;
    }

    if (wiringPiI2CReadReg16(i2c_fd, 0x81, data, 6) != 6) {
        perror("Error reading from Si1153");
        return -1;
    }

    *proximity = (data[0] << 8) | data[1];
    *alas = (data[2] << 8) | data[3];
    *uv = (data[4] << 8) | data[5];

    return 0;
}

int main() {
    int tint_level = 0;

    if ((i2c_fd = wiringPiI2CSetup(SI1153_I2C_ADDR)) < 0) {
        perror("Error initializing I2C");
        return 1;
    }

    if ((file_fd = open("/dev/null", O_WRONLY)) < 0) {
        perror("Error opening /dev/null");
        return 1;
    }

    while (1) {
        uint16_t proximity, alas, uv;

        if (si1153_read_data(&proximity, &alas, &uv) < 0) {
            continue;
        }

        log_data(fmt::format("Proximity: {}\tALS: {}\tUV: {}", proximity, alas, uv).c_str());

        if (uv > 800) {
            tint_level = 100;
        } else if (uv > 600) {
            tint_level = 75;
        } else if (uv > 400) {
            tint_level = 50;
        } else {
            tint_level = 0;
        }

        printf("Tint level: %d\n", tint_level);
        dup2(file_fd, STDOUT_FILENO);
        printf("Tint level: %d\n", tint_level);
        dup2(file_fd, STDERR_FILENO);
        system(fmt::format("setenv DISPLAY TINT_{}", tint_level).c_str());
        sleep(5);
    }

    return 0;
}
```

Replace the `setenv DISPLAY TINT_<tint_level>` command with the actual command to adjust the tint level on your Raspberry Pi's display.

To compile the program, use the following command:

```bash
gcc si1153_sensor.c -o si1153_sensor -lwiringPi -lrt
```

Finally, run the compiled program:

```bash
./si1153_sensor
```

This program initializes the I2C connection to the Si1153 sensor, reads proximity, ALS, and UV data, logs the data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file, and adjusts the tint level on the Raspberry Pi's display based on the UV index. Error handling is implemented for I2C communication failures.</s>

// Si1153 I2C address

#define SI1153_REG_ALS_VIS_DATA0 0x22
#define SI1153_REG_ALS_VIS_DATA1 0x23

#define SI1153_REG_PS1_DATA1 0x25
#define SI1153_REG_UV_INDEX0 0x33
#define SI1153_REG_UV_INDEX1 0x34

int fd;
FILE *log_file;

// Function to handle Ctrl+C signal
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("\nCaught signal %d, cleaning up...\n", signal);
    if (fd != -1) {
        close(fd);
    }
    if (log_file != NULL) {
        fclose(log_file);
    }
    exit(0);
}

// Function to read a 16-bit value from the sensor
int read_sensor_data(int fd, uint8_t reg) {
    int data = wiringPiI2CReadReg16(fd, reg);
    if (data == -1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error reading register 0x%02X\n", reg);
        return -1;
    }
    return data;
}

// Function to read sensor data and store it in a struct
int read_sensor_data_and_store(int fd, sensor_data_t *data) {
    int als_vis, ps1, uv_index;

    als_vis = read_sensor_data(fd, SI1153_REG_ALS_VIS_DATA0);
    if (als_vis == -1) return -1;
    ps1 = read_sensor_data(fd, SI1153_REG_PS1_DATA0);
    if (ps1 == -1) return -1;
    uv_index = read_sensor_data(fd, SI1153_REG_UV_INDEX0);
    if (uv_index == -1) return -1;

    data->als_visible = als_vis;
    data->proximity = ps1;
    data->uv_index = uv_index;

    return 0;
}

// Function to log sensor data to a file
void log_sensor_data(FILE *fp, sensor_data_t data) {
    time_t t = time(NULL);
    struct tm tm = *localtime(&t);
    fprintf(fp, "%d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d, ALS: %d, Proximity: %d, UV Index: %d\n",
            tm.tm_year + 1900, tm.tm_mon + 1, tm.tm_mday, tm.tm_hour, tm.tm_min, tm.tm_sec,
            data.als_visible, data.proximity, data.uv_index);
    fflush(fp);
}

int main() {
    sensor_data_t sensor_data;
    int ret;

    // Set up signal handling for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Initialize wiringPi and I2C
    wiringPiSetup();
    fd = wiringPiI2CSetup(SI1153_ADDR);
    if (fd == -1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error initializing I2C communication.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Open log file
    log_file = fopen("sensor_log.txt", "a");
    if (log_file == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error opening log file.\n");
        close(fd);
        return 1;
    }

    // Main loop
    while (1) {
        ret = read_sensor_data_and_store(fd, &sensor_data);
        if (ret == 0) {
            log_sensor_data(log_file, sensor_data);
            printf("Sensor data logged: ALS: %d, Proximity: %d, UV Index: %d\n",
                   sensor_data.als_visible, sensor_data.proximity, sensor_data.uv_index);
        } else {
            fprintf(stderr, "Error reading sensor data.\n");
        }

        delay(1000); // Delay for 1 second
    }

    return 0;
}

typedef struct {
    int als_visible;
    int proximity;
    int uv_index;
} sensor_data_t;</s>

input: Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C, reading proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index, logging the data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file; implement robust error handling for I2C communication failures and initialize with `wiringPiI2CSetup` for the Si1153 I2C address, simulating a smart window system adjusting tint based on UV.
prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the pigpio library to interface with the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C. The program should implement a motion-triggered surveillance system, logging proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file only when motion is detected by a PIR sensor connected to GPIO4.

Specifically:

1.  **Initialization:** Initialize the pigpio library using `gpioInitialise()`. If initialization fails, exit gracefully with an error message. Configure GPIO4 as an input using `gpioSetMode(4, PI_INPUT)`. Configure the Si1153 sensor using `i2cOpen()` with the appropriate I2C bus (typically 1) and the Si1153's I2C address (0x60). Implement error checking after `i2cOpen()` to ensure successful connection to the sensor.

2.  **Sensor Reading Functions:** Implement three functions to read the sensor data using a functional programming style (avoiding global state modification within the functions themselves).
    *   `readProximity(int handle)`: Reads the proximity value from the Si1153 using `i2cReadByteData()` with the appropriate register address. Handle potential I2C errors using `i2cReadByteData()` return value (negative values indicate errors).
    *   `readALS(int handle)`: Reads the ambient light sensor value from the Si1153 using `i2cReadWordData()` (or two `i2cReadByteData()` calls if necessary based on the sensor's register layout) and combines the bytes into a single 16-bit value. Handle potential I2C errors.
    *   `readUVIndex(int handle)`: Reads the UV index from the Si1153 using `i2cReadByteData()` with the appropriate register address. Handle potential I2C errors.

3.  **Motion Detection:** Continuously monitor the PIR sensor connected to GPIO4 using `gpioRead(4)`. If `gpioRead(4)` returns 1 (motion detected), proceed to read and log the sensor data.

4.  **Data Logging:** When motion is detected, get the current timestamp using `time()` and `localtime()`. Format the timestamp string using `strftime()`. Open the "sensor_log.txt" file in append mode ("a") using `fopen()`. Write the timestamp, proximity value, ALS value, and UV index to the file in a comma-separated format, using `fprintf()`. Close the file using `fclose()`. Include error handling for file operations (check the return value of `fopen()` and `fprintf()`).

5.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling for all I2C communication failures. If any `i2cReadByteData()` or `i2cReadWordData()` call returns a negative value, log an error message to the console using `fprintf(stderr, ...)` and attempt to re-initialize the I2C connection. If re-initialization fails after a few attempts, exit the program gracefully. Implement error handling for `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, and `fclose()` to ensure data integrity.

6.  **Cleanup:** Implement signal handling (e.g., using `signal(SIGINT, ...)` for Ctrl+C) to ensure proper cleanup before exiting. In the signal handler, close the I2C connection using `i2cClose()`, terminate the pigpio library using `gpioTerminate()`, and exit the program using `exit(0)`.

7.  **Functional Style:** The `readProximity`, `readALS`, and `readUVIndex` functions should operate without side effects (other than I/O). They should receive the I2C handle as an argument and return the sensor reading as a value.  Avoid global variables within these functions.

8.  **Standalone Approach:** The program should be self-contained and not rely on external configuration files or scripts. All necessary parameters (I2C address, GPIO pins) should be hardcoded or defined as constants.

9.  **Library Usage:** Use only the pigpio library and standard C libraries (stdio.h, stdlib.h, time.h, signal.h).

10. **Smart Window Tint Simulation (Simplified):** Although the original prompt mentioned a smart window, this will be simplified for this implementation.  After logging the data, print to the console a message indicating a simulated tint level adjustment based on the UV index. For example, if the UV index is above a certain threshold (e.g., 5), print "Simulating window tint darkening due to high UV index." This is a placeholder and does not require actual window tint control.

11. **Compilation:** The code should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag to link the pigpio library.

When motion is detected, it reads proximity, ALS, and UV index values from the Si1153 sensor, logs the data to a file with a timestamp, and simulates window tint adjustment based on the UV index. The code includes error handling for I2C communication and file operations, and it uses signal handling for graceful shutdown.

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <sys/ioctl.h>
#include <linux/i2c-dev.h>
#include <wiringPiI2C.h>
#include <time.h>

#define SI1153_ADDR 0x61 // Si1153 I2C address

int file;

void si1153_init() {
    if ((file = wiringPiI2CSetup(SI1153_ADDR)) < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to initialize I2C communication.\n");
        exit(1);
    }
}

unsigned char read_reg(unsigned char reg) {
    return WiringPiI2CReadReg16(file, reg);
}

void log_data(unsigned char proximity, unsigned short als, unsigned short uv) {
    time_t t = time(NULL);
    struct tm* time_info = localtime(&t);
    char timestamp[20];
    strftime(timestamp, sizeof(timestamp), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", time_info);

    FILE* log_file = fopen("sensor_log.txt", "a");
    if (log_file == NULL) {
        perror("Failed to open log file");
        return;
    }

    fprintf(log_file, "%s, Proximity: %d, ALS: %d, UV: %d\n", timestamp, proximity, als, uv);
    fclose(log_file);
}

int main() {
    si1153_init();

    // Main loop for reading and logging data
    while (1) {
        unsigned char proximity = read_reg(0x00);
        unsigned short als = read_reg(0x04) | (read_reg(0x05) << 8);
        unsigned short uv = read_reg(0x08) | (read_reg(0x09) << 8);

        log_data(proximity, als, uv);

        // Simulate a smart window system adjusting tint based on UV index
        if (uv > 500) {
            printf("High UV index! Increase window tint.\n");
            // Add your code to adjust the window tint based on the UV index
        }

        sleep(5); // Adjust the sleep interval as needed
    }

    return 0;
}
```

3. Compile and run the program:

```bash
gcc -o si1153_sensor si1153_sensor.c -lwiringPi -lwiringPiDev
./si1153_sensor
```

Make sure you have the Si1153 sensor correctly connected to your Raspberry Pi and the I2C address is set correctly in the `#define SI1153_ADDR 0x61` line. Adjust the sleep interval and the tint adjustment logic to suit your specific needs.</s>

 [/INST] To create a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 that reads data from the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C, logs the data with timestamps, and simulates a smart window system adjusting tint based on UV, you can follow the steps below. Note that this example assumes you have the wiringPi library installed.

1. Install the wiringPi library:

```bash
sudo apt-get update

2. Create a new file named `si1153_sensor.c` and paste the following code:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <wiringPiI2C.h>

#define SI1153_ADDR 0x62 // Si1153 I2C address
#define ALS_HI_RES 1
#define ALS_LOW_RES 0
#define UV_HI_RES 1
#define UV_LOW_RES 0

int main() {
    int file;
    struct timespec ts;
    int i2c_fd = wiringPiI2CSetup(SI1153_ADDR);

    if (i2c_fd < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to open I2C communication\n");
        return 1;
    }

    if ((file = open("sensor_log.txt", O_WRONLY | O_APPEND | O_CREAT, 0644)) < 0) {
        perror("Failed to open log file");
        return 1;
    }

    while (1) {
        // Read proximity value
        uint8_t proximity_data[2];
        if (wiringPiI2CReadReg16(i2c_fd, 0x04, proximity_data, 2) < 0) {
            perror("Failed to read proximity data");
            continue;
        }
        uint16_t proximity = (proximity_data[0] << 8) | proximity_data[1];

        // Read ALS value
        uint8_t als_data[2];
        if (wiringPiI2CReadReg16(i2c_fd, 0x82 + ALS_LOW_RES * 4, als_data, 2) < 0) {
            perror("Failed to read ALS data");
            continue;
        }
        uint16_t als = (als_data[0] << 8) | als_data[1];

        // Read UV value
        uint8_t uv_data[2];
        if (wiringPiI2CReadReg16(i2c_fd, 0x86 + UV_LOW_RES * 4, uv_data, 2) < 0) {
            perror("Failed to read UV data");
            continue;
        }
        uint16_t uv = (uv_data[0] << 8) | uv_data[1];

        // Format timestamp
        clock_gettime(CLOCK_REALTIME, &ts);
        char timestamp[30];
        snprintf(timestamp, sizeof(timestamp), "%ld.%09ld\n", ts.tv_sec, ts.tv_nsec);

        // Write data to log file
        if (write(file, timestamp, strlen(timestamp)) < 0 ||

input: Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C, reading proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index, logging the data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file; implement robust error handling for I2C communication failures and initialize with `wiringPiI2CSetup` for the Si1153 I2C address, simulating a smart window system adjusting tint based on UV.
prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the pigpio library to interface with the Si1153 Proximity/ALS/UV sensor via I2C. The program should implement a motion-triggered surveillance system, logging proximity, ambient light (ALS), and UV index data with timestamps to a "sensor_log.txt" file only when motion is detected by a PIR sensor connected to GPIO4.

Specifically:

1.  **Initialization:** Initialize the pigpio library using `gpioInitialise()`. If initialization fails, exit gracefully with an error message. Configure GPIO4 as an input using `gpioSetMode(4, PI_INPUT)`. Configure the Si1153 sensor using `i2cOpen()` with the appropriate I2C bus (typically 1) and the Si1153's I2C address (0x60). Implement error checking after `i2cOpen()` to ensure successful connection to the sensor.

2.  **Sensor Reading Functions:** Implement three functions to read the sensor data using a functional programming style (avoiding global state modification within the functions themselves).
    *   `readProximity(int handle)`: Reads the proximity value from the Si1153 using `i2cReadByteData()` with the appropriate register address. Handle potential I2C errors using `i2cReadByteData()` return value (negative values indicate errors).
    *   `readALS(int handle)`: Reads the ambient light sensor value from the Si1153 using `i2cReadWordData()` (or two `i2cReadByteData()` calls if necessary based on the sensor's register layout) and combines the bytes into a single 16-bit value. Handle potential I2C errors.
    *   `readUVIndex(int handle)`: Reads the UV index from the Si1153 using `i2cReadByteData()` with the appropriate register address. Handle potential I2C errors.

3.  **Motion Detection:** Continuously monitor the PIR sensor connected to GPIO4 using `gpioRead(4)`. If `gpioRead(4)` returns 1 (motion detected), proceed to read and log the sensor data.

4.  **Data Logging:** When motion is detected, get the current timestamp using `time()` and `localtime()`. Format the timestamp string using `strftime()`. Open the "sensor_log.txt" file in append mode ("a") using `fopen()`. Write the timestamp, proximity value, ALS value, and UV index to the file in a comma-separated format, using `fprintf()`. Close the file using `fclose()`. Include error handling for file operations (check the return value of `fopen()` and `fprintf()`).

5.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling for all I2C communication failures. If any `i2cReadByteData()` or `i2cReadWordData()` call returns a negative value, log an error message to the console using `fprintf(stderr, ...)` and attempt to re-initialize the I2C connection. If re-initialization fails after a few attempts, exit the program gracefully. Implement error handling for `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, and `fclose()` to ensure data integrity.

6.  **Cleanup:** Implement signal handling (e.g., using `signal(SIGINT, ...)` for Ctrl+C) to ensure proper cleanup before exiting. In the signal handler, close the I2C connection using `i2cClose()`, terminate the pigpio library using `gpioTerminate()`, and exit the program using `exit(0)`.

7.  **Functional Style:** The `readProximity`, `readALS`, and `readUVIndex` functions should operate without side effects (other than I/O). They should receive the I2C handle as an argument and return the sensor reading as a value.  Avoid global variables within these functions.

8.  **Standalone Approach:** The program should be self-contained and not rely on external configuration files or scripts. All necessary parameters (I2C address, GPIO pins) should be hardcoded or defined as constants.

9.  **Library Usage:** Use only the pigpio library and standard C libraries (stdio.h, stdlib.h, time.h, signal.h).

10. **Smart Window Tint Simulation (Simplified):** Although the original prompt mentioned a smart window, this will be simplified for this implementation.  After logging the data, print to the console a message indicating a simulated tint level adjustment based on the UV index. For example, if the UV index is above a certain threshold (e.g., 5), print "Simulating window tint darkening due to high UV index." This is a placeholder and does not require actual window tint control.

11. **Compilation:** The code should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag to link the pigpio library.
explanation: The code initializes the pigpio library, configures the PIR sensor GPIO as input, and opens an I2C connection to the Si1153 sensor. It continuously monitors the PIR sensor for motion. When motion is detected, it reads proximity, ALS, and UV index values from the Si1153 sensor, logs the data to a file with a timestamp, and simulates window tint adjustment based on the UV index. The code includes error handling for I2C communication and file operations, and it uses signal handling for graceful shutdown.

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install wiringPi
```

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <sys/ioctl.h>
#include <linux/i2c-dev.h>
#include <wiringPiI2C.h>

#define SI1153_ADDR 0x62 // Si1153 I2C address
#define ALS_REG 0x82
#define PROX_REG 0x84
#define UV_REG 0x86

int file;

void init() {
    if ((file = wiringPiI2CSetup(SI1153_ADDR)) < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to open I2C bus.\n");
        exit(1);
    }
}

unsigned short read_16(unsigned char reg) {
    unsigned char data[2];
    if (wiringPiI2CReadReg16(file, reg, data) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "I2C read error.\n");
        exit(1);
    }
    return (data[0] << 8) | data[1];
}

void log_data(unsigned short proximity, unsigned short als, unsigned short uv) {
    char timestr[30];
    time_t now = time(NULL);
    struct tm* tm_now = localtime(&now);
    strftime(timestr, sizeof(timestr), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", tm_now);

    FILE* logfile = fopen("sensor_log.txt", "a");
    if (!logfile) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to open log file.\n");
        return;
    }

    fprintf(logfile, "%s, Proximity: %d, ALS: %d, UV: %d\n", timestr, proximity, als, uv);
    fclose(logfile);
}

int main() {
    init();

    for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
        unsigned short proximity = read_16(PROX_REG);
        unsigned short als = read_16(ALS_REG);
        unsigned short uv = read_16(UV_REG);

        log_data(proximity, als, uv);

        // Simulate smart window system adjusting tint based on UV
        if (uv > 8) {
            printf("UV index is high, adjusting tint...\n");
            // Implement tint adjustment logic here
        }

        sleep(1); // Adjust delay as needed
    }

    return 0;
}
```

To compile the program:

```bash
gcc si1153_sensor.c -o si1153_sensor -lwiringPi
```

Run the compiled program:

```bash
sudo ./si1153_sensor
```

task: Create a External Interrupt From A Tilt Switch Interrupt_Driven Application for Home Automation
category: Safety Interlock System Monitor
input: Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 to monitor a safety interlock system using a ball tilt switch connected to GPIO17 (BCM numbering), logging the switch state changes with timestamps to a circular buffer in memory and displaying the last 10 events via UART using `stdio.h`. Implement robust error handling for GPIO initialization and potential interrupt issues.
prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 to monitor a safety interlock system within a home automation context using a ball tilt switch connected to GPIO17 (BCM numbering). Implement a distributed system approach where the Raspberry Pi 5 acts as an edge node, communicating status updates to a central server (simulated locally for testing purposes). The program must adhere to a functional programming style in C and utilize standard C libraries, avoiding C++ or non-standard libraries.

Specifically, the program should:

1.  **GPIO Handling:** Use the `wiringPi` library for GPIO control. Initialize GPIO17 as an input with pull-up resistor enabled using `wiringPiSetupGpio()` and `pinMode(17, INPUT)` and `pullUpDnControl(17, PUD_UP)`. Implement robust error handling using `if` statements to check the return values of `wiringPiSetupGpio()` and `pinMode()` and log any errors to `stderr` using `fprintf()` along with `errno` and `strerror()`. Exit gracefully if initialization fails.

2.  **Interrupt Handling:** Use `wiringPiISR(17, INT_EDGE_BOTH, &tiltSwitchInterruptHandler)` to register an interrupt handler for both rising and falling edges on GPIO17. The `tiltSwitchInterruptHandler` function should be concise and primarily responsible for updating a global volatile variable `tiltSwitchState` with the current digitalRead(17) value and calling the `logEvent` function.

3.  **Circular Buffer:** Implement a circular buffer in memory to store the last 10 interlock events. Each event should consist of a timestamp (obtained using `time(NULL)`) and the switch state (0 or 1). Use a `struct` to represent an event: `typedef struct { time_t timestamp; int state; } Event;` Allocate the circular buffer using `malloc`: `Event *circularBuffer = (Event*) malloc(10 * sizeof(Event));` Implement functions `enqueueEvent(Event event)` and `dequeueEvent()` to manage the circular buffer, handling wraparound correctly.

4.  **Logging:** The `logEvent(int state)` function should create an `Event` struct, populate it with the current timestamp and state, and enqueue it into the circular buffer.

5.  **UART Output:** Implement a function `displayLast10Events()` that retrieves the last 10 events from the circular buffer and displays them via UART using `printf()` and `stdio.h`. Format the output clearly, including timestamps and switch states. This function should be called periodically (e.g., every 5 seconds) using a timer.

6.  **Timer:** Use the `nanosleep()` function to create a 5-second timer. After each timer interval, call `displayLast10Events()`.

7.  **Functional Programming Style:** Structure the code using small, pure functions that perform specific tasks. Avoid global mutable state as much as possible. Pass data between functions explicitly. The main loop should primarily orchestrate calls to these functions.

8.  **Distributed System Simulation:** Implement a function `sendEventToServer(Event event)` that simulates sending the event data to a central server. For this simulation, simply print the event data to `stdout` using `printf()` in a format suitable for parsing by a server (e.g., JSON-like key-value pairs). Call this function from within `logEvent()`.

9.  **Error Handling:** Implement comprehensive error handling. Check the return values of all library functions (e.g., `wiringPiSetupGpio()`, `malloc()`, `time()`, `nanosleep()`) and log errors to `stderr` using `fprintf()` along with `errno` and `strerror()`. Free allocated memory before exiting on error.

10. **Signal Handling:** Implement signal handling for `SIGINT` (Ctrl+C) to gracefully shut down the program. In the signal handler, disable interrupts, free allocated memory, and exit cleanly. Use `signal(SIGINT, signalHandler)` to register the signal handler.

11. **Memory Management:** Ensure proper memory management. Free the circular buffer when the program exits.

12. **Concurrency:** Since `wiringPiISR` runs in a separate thread, ensure proper synchronization between the interrupt handler and the main loop when accessing shared resources (e.g., the circular buffer). While atomics are preferable, for simplicity, a volatile variable is allowed for the tiltSwitchState.

13. **Code Clarity:** Write clean, well-commented code that is easy to understand and maintain.

14. **Compile and Execution:** Provide clear instructions on how to compile and execute the program on a Raspberry Pi 5, including any necessary library installations (e.g., `sudo apt-get install libwiringpi-dev`).
explanation: The code initializes the GPIO pin, sets up an interrupt handler for the tilt switch, and implements a circular buffer to store event data. It simulates sending event data to a server and includes a timer to periodically display the last 10 events. Signal handling is implemented for graceful shutdown.
tags: external interrupt from a tilt switch
base_response:
<s><s>[INST]  Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 to monitor a safety interlock system using a ball tilt switch connected to GPIO17 (BCM numbering), logging the switch state changes with timestamps to a circular buffer in memory and displaying the last 10 events via UART using `stdio.h`. Implement robust error handling for GPIO initialization and potential interrupt issues.
 [/INST] I cannot write and test the entire code for you, but I can provide you with a basic outline and some guidance on how to approach this task. Here's a step-by-step guide to create a C program for Raspberry Pi that monitors a safety interlock system using a ball tilt switch connected to GPIO17 (BCM numbering):

1. Include necessary headers:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <sys/time.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>

#define BUFFER_SIZE 10
#define GPIO_PIN 17
```

2. Initialize the WiringPi library and set up the GPIO pin as an input:

```c
int main(void) {
    if (wiringPiSetup() == -1) {
        printf("Failed to initialize wiringPi!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    pinMode(GPIO_PIN, INPUT);

    // Initialize the circular buffer
    struct event_t {
        struct timeval timestamp;
        int state;
    } events[BUFFER_SIZE];
    memset(events, 0, BUFFER_SIZE * sizeof(struct event_t));
    int head = 0;
    int tail = 0;
```

3. Implement a function to log switch state changes with timestamps:

```c
void log_event(int state) {
    struct timeval tv;
    gettimeofday(&tv, NULL);
    events[head].timestamp = tv;
    events[head].state = state;
    head = (head + 1) % BUFFER_SIZE;
}
```

4. Set up an interrupt handler for the ball tilt switch:

```c
void interrupt_handler(int channel) {
    log_event(digitalRead(GPIO_PIN));
}
```

5. Configure the GPIO pin for edge detection and set up the interrupt:

```c
wiringPiISR(GPIO_PIN, INT_EDGE_BOTH, interrupt_handler);
```

6. Display the last 10 events via UART:

```c
void display_events() {
    printf("Last 10 events:\n");
    for (int i = tail; i != head; i = (i + 1) % BUFFER_SIZE) {
        printf("Event at %ld.%06lds: State = %d\n",
               events[i].timestamp.tv_sec, events[i].timestamp.tv_usec, events[i].state);
    }
}
```

7. Main loop to display events periodically:

```c
while (1) {
    display_events();
    sleep(5);
}

return 0;
}
```

8. Compile and run the program:

```sh
gcc -o safety_monitor safety_monitor.c -lwiringPi -o safety_monitor
./safety_monitor
```

This is a basic outline for the program. Make sure to test the ball tilt switch on the Raspberry Pi's GPIO17 before connecting it to your safety interlock system. You may also want to add error handling for potential interrupt issues, such as the interrupt handler being called concurrently. Additionally, you may want to consider implementing a mechanism to reset the circular buffer if it becomes full, or handle cases where the program restarts and needs to catch up on missed events.</s>

<s><s>[INST]  Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 to monitor a safety interlock system using a ball tilt switch connected to GPIO17 (BCM numbering), logging the switch state changes with timestamps to a circular buffer in memory and displaying the last 10 events via UART using `stdio.h`. Implement robust error handling for GPIO initialization and potential interrupt issues.
 [/INST]  #include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Define the GPIO pin for the tilt switch
#define TILT_SWITCH_PIN 17

// Define the circular buffer size
#define BUFFER_SIZE 10

// Define the UART device
#define UART_DEVICE "/dev/ttyAMA0"

// Global variables
int uart_handle;
volatile int running = 1;

// Structure to hold timestamp and state
typedef struct {
    time_t timestamp;
    int state;
} Event;

// Circular buffer
Event event_buffer[BUFFER_SIZE];
int buffer_head = 0;
int buffer_count = 0;

// Function prototypes
int initialize_gpio();
int initialize_uart();
void interrupt_handler(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick, void *userdata);
void log_event(int state);
void display_last_events();
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Initialize the GPIO pin
int initialize_gpio() {
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    gpioSetMode(TILT_SWITCH_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(TILT_SWITCH_PIN, PI_PUD_UP); // Enable pull-up resistor

    // Set interrupt on GPIO pin
    if (gpioSetISRFuncEx(TILT_SWITCH_PIN, RISING_EDGE, 0, interrupt_handler, NULL) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set interrupt for GPIO %d\n", TILT_SWITCH_PIN);
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    return 0;
}

// Initialize the UART device
int initialize_uart() {
    uart_handle = fopen(UART_DEVICE, "r+");
    if (uart_handle == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to open UART device %s\n", UART_DEVICE);
        return 1;
    }

    // Configure UART (9600 baud, 8N1)
    int baud_rate = 9600;
    int data_bits = 8;
    int stop_bits = 1;
    int flow_control = 0;

    if (fcntl(uart_handle, F_SETFD, 0) == -1) {
        perror("fcntl F_SETFD");
        return 1;
    }

    if (cfsetospeed(&uart_handle, baud_rate) != 0) {
        perror("cfsetospeed");
        return 1;
    }
    if (cfsetispeed(&uart_handle, baud_rate) != 0) {
        perror("cfsetispeed");
        return 1;
    }

    if (cfsetgid(&uart_handle, 0) != 0) {
        perror("cfsetgid");
        return 1;
    }

    if (cfsetpcr(&uart_handle, 0) != 0) {
        perror("cfsetpcr");
        return 1;
    }

    if (cfsetflags(&uart_handle, IGNBRK | BRKINT | PARMRK | ISTRIP | INLCR | IGNPAR | ICANON | ECHO | ECHOE | ISIG) != 0) {
        perror("cfsetflags");
        return 1;
    }

    if (cfsetcflag(&uart_handle, data_bits | stop_bits | flow_control) != 0) {
        perror("cfsetcflag");
        return 1;
    }

    return 0;
}

// Interrupt handler for GPIO pin
void interrupt_handler(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick, void *userdata) {
    if (level == 1) { // Rising edge detected
        log_event(1); // State 1 (Tilt detected)
    } else {
        log_event(0); // State 0 (No tilt)
    }
}

// Log an event to the circular buffer
void log_event(int state) {
    time_t now = time(NULL);
    event_buffer[buffer_head].timestamp = now;
    event_buffer[buffer_head].state = state;

    buffer_head = (buffer_head + 1) % BUFFER_SIZE;
    if (buffer_count < BUFFER_SIZE) {
        buffer_count++;
    }
}

// Display the last 10 events via UART
void display_last_events() {
    printf("Last %d Events:\n", buffer_count);
    for (int i = 0; i < buffer_count; i++) {
        int index = (buffer_head - buffer_count + i + BUFFER_SIZE) % BUFFER_SIZE;
        struct tm *timeinfo = localtime(&event_buffer[index].timestamp);
        char timestamp_str[20];
        strftime(timestamp_str, sizeof(timestamp_str), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", timeinfo);
        printf("[%s] State: %s\n", timestamp_str, event_buffer[index].state ? "Tilt Detected" : "No Tilt");
    }
    printf("\n");
}

// Signal handler for graceful shutdown
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("Caught signal %d, exiting gracefully...\n", signal);
    running = 0;
    if (uart_handle != NULL) {
        fclose(uart_handle);
    }
    gpioTerminate();
    exit(0);
}

int main() {
    // Register signal handler
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Initialize GPIO
    if (initialize_gpio() != 0) {
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize UART
    if (initialize_uart() != 0) {
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    printf("Safety Interlock System Monitoring Started.\n");

    // Main loop
    while (running) {
        display_last_events();
        gpioDelay(100); // Delay to avoid excessive UART output
    }

    // Cleanup (should not reach here due to signal handler)
    fclose(uart_handle);
    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}

// Compilation instructions:
// gcc -o safety_interlock safety_interlock.c -lpigpio -lrt -lcurses -ltermcap
// sudo ./safety_interlock
// Note: The program requires root privileges to access UART.
// To make the program runnable by a non-root user, you can create a udev rule to allow access to the UART device.
// For example, create /etc/udev/rules.d/51-serial.rules and add:
// SUBSYSTEM=="tty", KERNEL=="ttyAMA0", OWNER="pi", GROUP="dialout"
// Then, add the user to the dialout group: sudo usermod -a -G dialout pi
